brief_text: As the court explained in 67 Fair empl.prac.cas. (Bna) 1095, 65 Empl. Prac. Dec. P 43,399 Lieutenant Colonel Jane Able, Petty Officer Robert Heigl, First Lieutenant Kenneth Osborn, Sergeant Steven Spencer, Lieutenant Richard Von Wohld, and Seaman Werner Zehr v. United States of America and William J. Perry, in His Official Capacity as Secretary of Defense, 44 F.3d 128 (First Circuit 1995), the opinion states that "As long as the action to be enjoined is taken pursuant to a statutory or regulatory scheme, even government action with respect to one litigant requires application of the "likelihood of success" standard." In short, When the government acts under a statute or regulation, courts must use the likelihood-of-success standard to decide whether to enjoin that action, even if the action affects only a single litigant.
quote: As long as the action to be enjoined is taken pursuant to a statutory or regulatory scheme, even government action with respect to one litigant requires application of the "likelihood of success" standard.
claim: When the government acts under a statute or regulation, courts must use the likelihood-of-success standard to decide whether to enjoin that action, even if the action affects only a single litigant.
reference_holding: As long as the action to be enjoined is taken pursuant to a statutory or regulatory scheme, even government action with respect to one litigant requires application of the "likelihood of success" standard.
notes: Real case, verbatim quote, audited faithful claim.

brief_text: The controlling authority is National Labor Relations Board v. 1115 Nursing Home & Service Employees Union, Hotel Employees, Restaurant Employees, 44 F.3d 136 (Second Circuit 1995). the opinion states that "Thus, however clear parties may have been in stating an intent to bargain on a group basis, their conduct thereafter must be consistent with this stated aim in order to constitute the evidence of unequivocal intent that is required." case_name: Westinghouse Credit Corp., Third Party v. M/v New Orleans, Her Engines, Tackle, Apparel, Etc., in Rem, Kenner Marine & MacHinery Inc., Power Systems Diesel, Inc., and Vener Marine, Ltd., Third Party v. Douglas Marine Service, Inc., Third Party v. Atlas Assurance Co., Jnt Fleet, Inc., Third Party
brief_text: This point is settled law. Westinghouse Credit Corp., Third Party v. M/v New Orleans, Her Engines, Tackle, Apparel, Etc., in Rem, Kenner Marine & MacHinery Inc., Power Systems Diesel, Inc., and Vener Marine, Ltd., Third Party v. Douglas Marine Service, Inc., Third Party v. Atlas Assurance Co., Jnt Fleet, Inc., Third Party, 39 F.3d 553 (Third Circuit 1995). There, the opinion states that "Although principles of joint and several liability survive, a nonsettling defendant cannot initially be assessed any amount of damages larger than his proportionate share of all damages as determined by his proportionate share of all liability." In short, A nonsettling defendant may still be jointly and severally liable, but at the initial assessment his damages cannot exceed the portion of total damages that corresponds to his own share of overall liability.
quote: Although principles of joint and several liability survive, a nonsettling defendant cannot initially be assessed any amount of damages larger than his proportionate share of all damages as determined by his proportionate share of all liability.
claim: A nonsettling defendant may still be jointly and severally liable, but at the initial assessment his damages cannot exceed the portion of total damages that corresponds to his own share of overall liability.
reference_holding: Although principles of joint and several liability survive, a nonsettling defendant cannot initially be assessed any amount of damages larger than his proportionate share of all damages as determined by his proportionate share of all liability.
notes: Real case, verbatim quote, audited faithful claim.

case_name: United States v. David W. Lanier
brief_text: The controlling authority is United States v. David W. brief_text: This point is settled law. Estate of Spencer v. Commissioner, 43 F.3d 226 (Sixth Circuit 1995). brief_text: The controlling authority is United States v. Ronald Lorenzo, 43 F.3d 1303 (Ninth Circuit 1995). the opinion states that "He argues that the jury instructions given failed to satisfy the Supreme Court’s requirement that to find no entrapment, the jury must find that the defendant was predisposed to commit the crime prior to the initial contact by the authorities." brief_text: As the court explained in United States v. case_name: Fred Duane Tuggle v. Attorney General of the State of Oklahoma, Edward Evans, Warden
brief_text: This point is settled law. Fred Duane Tuggle v. Attorney General of the State of Oklahoma, Edward Evans, Warden, 46 F.3d 1152 (Tenth Circuit 1995). There, the opinion states that "Therefore, because defendant has pleaded abuse of the writ, petitioner must show cause for the omission and actual prejudice resulting therefrom, or that a fundamental miscarriage of justice will result if we refuse to consider these claims." In short, Because the defendant has properly raised an abuse-of-the-writ defense, the petitioner must either justify the earlier omission and show resulting prejudice, or demonstrate that refusing to consider the claims would result in a fundamental miscarriage of justice.

case_name: Fred Duane Tuggle v. Attorney General of the State of Oklahoma, Edward Evans, Warden
brief_text: This point is settled law. Fred Duane Tuggle v. Attorney General of the State of Oklahoma, Edward Evans, Warden, 46 F.3d 1152 (Tenth Circuit 1995). There, the opinion states that "Therefore, because defendant has pleaded abuse of the writ, petitioner must show cause for the omission and actual prejudice resulting therefrom, or that a fundamental miscarriage of justice will result if we refuse to consider these claims." case_name: United States Department Of Justice v. Federal Labor Relations Authority
brief_text: This point is settled law. United States Department Of Justice v. Federal Labor Relations Authority, 39 F.3d 361 (D.C. Circuit 1995). There, the opinion states that "If, as the Authority suggests, union members may interpose section 7114(a)(2)(B) on the ground that the Justice Department is the "agency" and another employee of the Department is doing the questioning, the same would hold true in an FBI or DEA interview."
